petitioner_background: The petitioner is a goalkeeper director and coach seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA section 203(b)(1)(A)). He intends to continue his activities as a goalkeeper director and coach in the United States. He had previously been granted O-1 nonimmigrant status (a classification reserved for nonimmigrants of extraordinary ability). His nationality is not explicitly stated in the decision.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted articles about himself along with evidence regarding circulation statistics for the publications in which the articles appeared. Some of the articles did not contain a legible URL and some did not list an author, while other articles did identify an author.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the Director's decision did not reflect a full review of the evidence. The Director had concluded that articles without legible URLs were unreliable and given no evidentiary weight, and that articles without listed authors did not warrant consideration. The AAO agreed with the petitioner that the Director erred by not affording individual consideration to articles that did identify an author, and that the Director's 'bare-boned' analysis erroneously concluded the articles were not supported by evidence of circulation statistics when such evidence had in fact been submitted and ignored. The AAO found the Director did not provide sufficient explanation for the blanket rejection of the evidence and instructed re-evaluation on remand.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a certificate from the City stating that the petitioner served as a judge for four years on a panel of seven professionals, a letter from the Town Hall attesting to this role as well as a copy of their rules regarding jury selection, and other supporting evidence.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the petitioner that it was difficult to discern from the Director's decision what specific evidence was considered. The Director's decision only vaguely concluded that 'simply performing one's job-related duties demonstrates competency but is not evidence that his achievements have been recognized in the field of expertise,' without specifically identifying the evidence considered. The AAO instructed the Director to re-examine the petitioner's claims and all evidence submitted in support of those claims when evaluating this criterion on remand.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted eleven letters and nine supporting exhibits in support of his claim that he served in a leading or critical role for organizations or establishments with a distinguished reputation. The evidence related to his participation in the creation and execution of training methodology, improvement of the level of goalkeeping methodology and scouting network, and his distinguished treatment of players.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the Director misapplied the law by stating that the petitioner did not provide detailed information regarding how his role was leading or critical for an 'entire organization.' The AAO noted that USCIS policy states that the leading or critical role may be 'for an organization, establishment, or a division or department of an organization or establishment' per 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.2(B)(1). The AAO also agreed that the Director's single sentence analysis indicated the eleven letters and nine supporting exhibits were not fully considered and lacked specific discussion. The AAO instructed re-evaluation on remand.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted an offer of employment showing prospective wages as evidence that he commanded a high salary or other significantly high remuneration for services in relation to others in the field.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the petitioner that the Director erred. The Director's decision stated that 'an employment offer is not evidence of wages earned because it is not always paid out. Therefore this evidence cannot be included in the salary calculations.' The AAO held that, contrary to the Director's determination, USCIS does not interpret the phrase 'has commanded' to mean that the person must have already earned such salary or remuneration to meet the criterion. Per 6 USCIS Policy Manual F.2(B)(1), a credible job offer showing prospective wages may establish a petitioner's ability to command a high salary in their field. The AAO remanded for the Director to re-examine the evidence under this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO withdrew the Director's decision and remanded the matter for entry of a new decision. Although the Director's decision contained a brief analysis for each of the four criteria addressed, the discussions contained few or no references to the specific evidence considered. The AAO found that the petitioner was not adequately informed of the Director's reasons for determining that none of the materials satisfied the regulatory requirements, and that an officer must fully explain the reasons for denying a petition to allow a fair opportunity to contest the decision and meaningful appellate review. The AAO also found legal errors in the Director's analysis of the leading/critical role and high salary criteria. The AAO additionally noted that the prior approval of an O-1 nonimmigrant petition does not preclude denial of an immigrant petition adjudicated under a different standard.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Q Data Consulting, Inc. v. INS, 293 F. Supp. 2d 25 (D.D.C. 2003)
IKEA US v. US Dept. of Justice, 48 F. Supp. 2d 22 (D.D.C. 1999)
Fedin Bros. Co., Ltd. v. Sava, 724 F. Supp. 1103 (E.D.N.Y. 1989), aff'd, 905 F.2d 41 (2d Cir. 1990)
La. Philharmonic Orchestra v. INS, No. 98-2855, 2000 WL 282785 (E.D. La. 2000)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue: The AAO found that the Director's decision was insufficient for meaningful appellate review because it failed to adequately explain the reasons for denial. The Director's analysis contained few or no references to the specific evidence considered, gave only vague or 'bare-boned' conclusions, and did not afford individual consideration to submitted documents. The AAO also identified legal errors: the Director misapplied the standard for the leading/critical role criterion by requiring the role to relate to an 'entire organization,' and misapplied the high salary criterion by requiring evidence of wages actually earned rather than accepting a credible prospective job offer. No fraud or misrepresentation findings.
